candidates.summary: King County Superior Court judge appointed by Governor Inslee in 2022 and twice elected since, after roughly 20 years as a public defender and ACLU of Washington legal strategist. She is endorsed by sitting Governor Ferguson and more than 100 judges.
highlights: Says justices must apply the rule of law "based on our constitution, our statutes ... precedent ... not personal views," while remaining open to reform [S4]
First ACLU of Washington attorney to become a judge, after a career in state and federal indigent defense [S1]
Has presided over hundreds of criminal, civil, and family cases since 2022; her pamphlet states no trial has been overturned for legal error [S1]
Endorsed by Governor Ferguson, Justice Whitener, retired Justice Yu, and 100+ current and retired judges [S1]

judicial.basis: Stakes a middle framing of constitution, statute, and precedent over personal views, but pairs it with openness to reform and a rights-and-access emphasis rooted in her civil-liberties career.

safety.basis: Professional formation is squarely on the defense/civil-liberties side: ~20 years as a state and federal public defender and ACLU of Washington attorney; no explicit bail or diversion positions documented.

experience.basis: Sitting Superior Court judge since 2022, twice elected, with hundreds of cases presided — a proven-record profile, though with shorter bench tenure than Diaz or Stevens.

sources.note: confirms current judgeship; page shows contact/courtroom info only

candidates.name: David Stevens
candidates.summary: Mason County Superior Court judge who reached the bench by election rather than appointment, after a career spanning prosecution, public defense, and international rule-of-law work in Kosovo and Afghanistan. He describes himself as a strict constructionist and holds Republican party recommendations.
highlights: Self-described "strict constructionist": statutes read literally, and the constitution interpreted "based on its text and original meaning and not on evolving societal standards" [S2]
Says public policy "is the province of the political branches and not the courts" and argues his election would bring balance to a court he sees as progressive-leaning [S1]
Career spans senior felony prosecution, federal public defense, tribal chief public defense, and international justice missions, with 200+ jury trials as lawyer and judge [S1]
Only candidate who reached the bench by election rather than gubernatorial appointment, which he makes a central argument [S1]
Recommended by the state, King County, and Snohomish County Republican parties; trails the field in fundraising [S3]

judicial.basis: Repeated explicit self-description as a strict constructionist committed to text and original meaning, judicial restraint, and courts staying out of policy-making.

safety.basis: Supports issuing warrants for defendants who fail to appear and opposes race-based jury selection, with law-enforcement (WACOPS) support; his mixed prosecutor/defender formation tempers the placement.

experience.basis: Long proven trial record (25+ years, 200+ jury trials, sitting elected judge) paired with an explicit outsider campaign against the appointment pipeline and the court's current direction.

candidates.name: Mike Diaz
candidates.summary: Washington Court of Appeals judge (Division I) and former federal civil-rights prosecutor, twice appointed to the bench by Governor Inslee. He describes his judicial philosophy as "American pragmatism" and leads the race in fundraising and establishment endorsements.
highlights: Only candidate with appellate-bench experience: has authored 200+ Court of Appeals opinions after four years as a trial judge [S1]
Describes his judicial philosophy as "American pragmatism" — law must "meet the community where they are" [S3]
Founded the Civil Rights Unit at the Seattle U.S. Attorney's Office and helped lead Seattle police reform efforts [S1]
Endorsed by three former governors, the attorney general, and nine current and retired Supreme Court justices; self-reports "Exceptionally Well Qualified" ratings from multiple bar associations [S1]
Leads the field in fundraising with over $220,000 raised as of early July [S3]

judicial.basis: Explicitly espouses "American pragmatism" with law meeting "the community where they are," an adaptive, community-responsive approach; pamphlet stresses protecting rights and freedoms.

safety.basis: Professional formation is mixed: 10 years as a federal prosecutor, but focused on founding a civil-rights unit and leading Seattle police-reform work rather than conventional enforcement.

experience.basis: Sitting appellate judge with 200+ authored opinions, eight years across two benches, and the race's broadest establishment endorsement roster — the maximal proven-record profile.

contests.office_does: One of nine statewide-elected justices on Washington's court of last resort, deciding appeals, state constitutional questions, and the rules that govern every court in the state.
contests.race_blurb: A special election to fill retired Justice Barbara Madsen's term, and the most crowded of the 2026 Supreme Court races: Justice Theo Angelis, appointed by Governor Ferguson in March 2026, faces retired Federal Way Municipal Court Judge Dave Larson, Thurston County Superior Court Judge Sharonda Amamilo, and veteran appellate attorney Greg Miller. Voters are effectively choosing among appellate craft, long trial-bench tenure, and current trial-court judging, with the top two advancing.

candidates.name: Greg Miller
candidates.summary: Seattle appellate attorney and Carney Badley Spellman partner with a 40-year Washington appellate practice — over 135 appeals and 80 oral arguments — but no judicial office beyond pro tem service. He frames the race around irreplaceable appellate craft and independence from the appointment process.
highlights: 40-year Washington appellate practice: briefing in 135+ appeals, 80+ oral arguments, and 25 amicus briefs in the state Supreme Court [S1][S2]
Describes taking cases individually without predetermined conclusions and applying the law fairly; says he is "not in anyone's pocket" [S3]
Expresses concern that unchecked gubernatorial appointment power can undermine judicial independence [S3]
Has never held elected judicial office; judging experience is limited to Seattle Municipal Court pro tem service [S1][S2]
Lists no endorsements and has raised about $22,000, a small fraction of the incumbent's total [S2][S4]

experience.basis: Mixed profile: a deep 40-year professional appellate record is his central pitch, yet he has never held judicial office and runs as an independent outsider to the appointment pipeline with no endorsements.

sources.outlet: Washington State Public Disclosure Commission (contributions dataset)

candidates.name: Theo Angelis
candidates.summary: Sitting Supreme Court justice appointed by Governor Ferguson in March 2026 after 26 years as a K&L Gates appellate and IP partner, with extensive pro bono immigration work. He runs with endorsements from eight sitting justices and three governors but only months of bench record.
highlights: On the Supreme Court since April 4, 2026, with no authored opinions identified as of mid-July — roughly three months of bench record [S4]
26 years leading appellate practice at K&L Gates; pro bono litigation secured government-paid lawyers for children in immigration proceedings [S1]
Says he has "fought for the rule of law, the rights of all individuals" and courts that are "fair, independent, and accessible" [S1]
Endorsed by eight sitting justices, Governors Ferguson, Inslee, and Gregoire, and the Washington State Labor Council; leads the field in fundraising [S3]
Rated "Exceptionally Well Qualified" by Washington Women Lawyers [S6]

experience.basis: The sitting justice with a 26-year appellate career and near-unanimous institutional support, though his bench tenure is only about three months by appointment rather than election.

sources.outlet: Washington State Public Disclosure Commission (contributions dataset)

candidates.name: Dave Larson
candidates.summary: Retired Federal Way Municipal Court judge with 18 years on the bench, making his fourth run for the Supreme Court after losing the 2024 race by under one percentage point. He runs as an explicit critic of the current court's direction, with the state Republican Party's recommendation and Forward Party support.
highlights: 18 years as an elected municipal-court judge (16 as presiding judge) plus 23 years as a civil trial lawyer; retained by voters four times [S3]
Calls the current court "a ship lost at sea," criticizes the Blake drug-possession decision as "motivated by ideology," and opposes proposed bail rules he says would cripple trial courts [S5]
Says judges should not legislate from the bench even when they personally disagree with a law [S4]
Served years on the DMCJA Therapeutic Courts Committee and describes trial-court approaches to crime and addiction combining "compassion and accountability" [S3]
Recommended by the Washington State Republican Party and endorsed by the Forward Party; second in fundraising at roughly $232,000 [S4]

judicial.basis: Repeated restraint-and-depoliticization themes — judges must not legislate from the bench, Blake was ideologically motivated, restore trust by rising above partisan interests — without an explicit originalist framework.

safety.basis: Genuinely mixed documented record: opposes proposed bail rules restricting warrant issuance, yet served six years on the Therapeutic Courts Committee and campaigns on combining compassion and accountability for crime and addiction.

experience.basis: Longest judicial tenure in the field (18 years, municipal level) but campaigns as an outside change agent against the current court and the appointment pipeline.

candidates.name: Sharonda Amamilo
candidates.summary: Thurston County Superior Court judge, former 12-year public defender, and retired Army intelligence chief warrant officer, elected in 2020 as the first African American judge in Thurston County history. She emphasizes precedent, trial-court perspective, and carries the race's most extensive bar-association ratings.
highlights: Sitting superior-court judge since 2021 handling felony trials, complex civil, family, and dependency dockets, plus Court of Appeals pro tem work [S2]
Emphasizes adherence to precedent for stability, with a "very high bar" for revisiting it, and stresses textual precision in statutory interpretation [S4]
News coverage reports she is known for scrutinizing bail and sentencing recommendations, particularly where disparities appear racially motivated [S5]
Rated "Exceptionally Well Qualified" by five minority and specialty bar associations and "Well Qualified" by a sixth [S1]
Endorsed by Justice Whitener, former Justice Yu, labor organizations, and numerous Democratic organizations, but trails badly in fundraising at roughly $35,600 [S2][S3]

judicial.basis: Genuinely mixed placement: precedent-and-stability emphasis with textual precision ("Words matter") on one side, and access-to-justice, consistency, and child-and-family protection priorities on the other.

safety.basis: Twelve years as a public defender plus press reports that she scrutinizes bail and sentencing recommendations for racially motivated disparities.

experience.basis: Runs on a proven current trial-bench record — sitting superior-court judge since 2021 with weekly trial work and top bar evaluations — rather than an outsider case.

contests.office_does: One of nine statewide-elected justices on Washington's court of last resort; this seat's incumbent additionally serves as Chief Justice, presiding over the court and leading administration of the state judiciary.
contests.race_blurb: The most asymmetric of the 2026 Supreme Court races: Chief Justice Debra Stephens, on the court since 2008 and author of the McCleary school-funding decision, faces three challengers — public-defender-turned-solo-attorney Karim Merchant, tax and business attorney Todd Bloom, and solo family/tax lawyer David Shelvey — none of whom has held judicial office. Institutional endorsements and fundraising are one-sided in the incumbent's favor.

candidates.name: David R Shelvey
candidates.summary: Solo-practice attorney from Sumner handling family, estate, tax, and civil matters, making his second Supreme Court run after drawing 3.4% in a 2024 primary. His pamphlet statement takes unusually outcome-oriented positions for a judicial race and he reports no judicial experience and no campaign contributions.
highlights: States in his pamphlet that he opposes a state income tax and "won't be soft on criminals" — outcome-oriented positions unusual in a nonpartisan judicial race [S1]
Reports no judicial experience, which he characterizes as "the same as many lawyers have when they take to the bench" [S1]
Eleven years of solo practice spanning family law, estate planning, taxation, and civil litigation [S1]
Received 3.4% statewide in the 2024 Supreme Court Position 2 primary; deliberately lists no endorsements to preserve impartiality [S4][S3]
PDC records show no reported contributions for the 2026 cycle [S5]

experience.basis: Solo practitioner with no judicial experience, no endorsements, no reported contributions, and a prior 3.4% statewide run — the field's fullest outsider profile.

safety.basis: His pamphlet pledge that he 'won't be soft on criminals' is a direct, documented enforcement-side posture toward criminal defendants — far more probative for the safety axis than for judicial philosophy, where the scorer placed it. Single clear statement supports medium; flagged for the merger because it is a stated posture rather than the bail/diversion/formation evidence the guide enumerates.

candidates.name: Debra L. Stephens
candidates.summary: Sitting Chief Justice of the Washington Supreme Court, on the court since 2008 after Court of Appeals service and a career as an appellate attorney and Gonzaga law professor. She authored the landmark McCleary school-funding decision and runs on judicial independence and access to justice.
highlights: 18 years on the Supreme Court, currently in a second tenure as Chief Justice, including leading the judiciary's COVID-19 response [S4]
Authored the majority opinion in McCleary v. State (2012), holding that the constitution confers a positive right to an amply funded K-12 education [S5]
States her core commitments as "an independent judiciary, courts that are open and accessible to everyone, and decisions grounded in law, not politics" [S2]
Endorsed by all eight other sitting justices, five retired justices, more than 150 judges, Governor Ferguson, and legislative leaders [S3]
Holds the race's only substantial war chest (~$83,000); her ballot opponent reported zero contributions [S6]

judicial.basis: Verified author of McCleary, reading the state constitution as guaranteeing a positive, enforceable right to funded education; platform pairs that rights-protective record with law-not-politics framing.

experience.basis: Sitting Chief Justice with 18 years on the court, prior Court of Appeals service, a landmark opinion record, and endorsement of the entire remaining bench — the maximal proven-record case.

candidates.name: Karim A. Merchant
candidates.summary: Criminal defense attorney who spent 15 of his 18 career years as a King County public defender and now runs a solo practice. He challenges the Chief Justice on a follow-the-Constitution, anti-establishment platform with no endorsements and minimal fundraising.
highlights: States his judicial philosophy as "follow the Constitution": courts should interpret law as written rather than inserting subjective intent [S3]
Says the current court has an "establishment culture," "clearly leans left," and criticized justices' participation in Pride flag ceremonies as creating an appearance of prejudgment [S3]
Disagreed with the Quinn capital-gains-tax ruling but supported the Blake drug-possession decision as constitutionally sound [S3]
15 years as a King County public defender; says he has never represented corporations or the government [S1]
No judicial service, no endorsements, and about $9,800 raised; frames both as marks of independence [S1][S4]

judicial.basis: Repeated explicit as-written philosophy — interpret law without subjective intent, keep courts free of politics — plus criticism of the court's perceived left lean and Quinn, applied consistently even where it favors defendants (Blake).

safety.basis: Professional formation is 15 years of public defense focused on protecting people from unlawful government intrusion, and he supported the Blake decision striking the drug-possession statute.

experience.basis: No judicial service and no endorsements by design; explicitly frames his candidacy as challenging the establishment from outside the appointment and endorsement system.

sources.outlet: The Center Square (republished by Everett Post)

candidates.name: Todd A. Bloom
candidates.summary: Tax and business attorney (CPA, CFA, CFP), former FINRA arbitrator, Navy veteran, and WSBA Board of Governors member challenging the Chief Justice. He argues justices owe loyalty to the Constitution rather than making law and carries the state Republican Party's recommendation.
highlights: Says judges owe loyalty to the Constitution and should defend it rather than make law, and frames the race as a choice to bring "meaningful new perspective" to balance the court [S3][S1]
Called the Quinn capital-gains ruling "wrongly decided," praising the dissent, and criticized the Blake drug-possession decision as undermining public trust [S3]
Two decades in tax, business, and securities law plus FINRA arbitration (1999-2020); no judicial service [S1]
Elected WSBA Board of Governors member (District 6) since 2023; 11 years as an active-duty Navy officer after 9/11 [S2]
Recommended by the Washington State Republican Party; minimal fundraising (~$4,700) [S4][S5]

judicial.basis: Constitution-loyalty, judges-should-not-make-law framing plus explicit criticism of both Quinn and Blake and a stated goal of balancing the court.

safety.basis: Criticized the Blake drug-possession decision, saying its outcome astonishes average citizens and undermines trust — an enforcement-side posture on the race's main criminal-law question.

experience.basis: No judicial service and a candidacy explicitly premised on bringing new perspective and common sense to the bench from outside the judiciary.

sources.outlet: Washington State Bar Association (Board of Governors bio)
The Center Square (republished by Everett Post)

contests.office_does: A King County District Court judge in the Northeast Electoral District handles the county's limited-jurisdiction docket — misdemeanor criminal calendars, traffic and other infractions, civil protection orders, and small-claims cases — from the Redmond-area courthouse.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat following Judge Marcus Nailor's retirement has drawn three candidates who all already sit pro tem in this court, so the choice turns on professional formation: Jan Trasen's 26 years in public defense and appellate work plus full-time judging as an ALJ, Josh Schaer's Assistant Attorney General career with pro tem service since 2008, and Bianca Tse's 16 years as a King County prosecutor. All three self-report top bar ratings.

candidates.name: Josh Schaer
candidates.summary: Assistant Attorney General with 25 years of courtroom experience spanning public defense, civil practice, and appellate advising, and a King County judge pro tem since 2008. He is the only candidate with prior elected office, having served eight years on the Issaquah City Council.
highlights: Longest part-time judicial service in the race: judge pro tem in King County courts since 2008 [S1]
Career lane is government civil/appellate practice at the Attorney General's Office, with earlier public defense [S1][S2]
Only candidate with prior elected office — eight years on the Issaquah City Council [S2]

Self-reports "Exceptionally Well-Qualified" ratings from four bar associations including KCBA (unverified); endorsed by the 1st LD Democrats, AG Nick Brown, and Executive Zahilay per his statement [S1]

experience.basis: Proven-record profile: 18 years of pro tem judicial service, 25 years of courtroom practice, and eight years in elected office.

sources.ref: PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

candidates.name: Jan Trasen
candidates.summary: Administrative Law Judge with the state Office of Administrative Hearings and King County District Court pro tem, after 26 years as a public defender and appellate attorney in Washington and New York. She holds the King County Democrats endorsement and campaigns on treatment-focused approaches to reduce recidivism.
highlights: The only candidate whose day job is full-time judging (ALJ, Child & Family Care Division), alongside pro tem service in this court [S1][S2]
26 years as a public defender and appellate attorney, arguing criminal and dependency cases before the Washington Supreme Court and Court of Appeals [S1]
Advocates mental-health and substance-use treatment programs designed to lower recidivism and strengthen neighborhood safety [S2]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats (independently confirmed) and, per her statement, a majority of the Washington Supreme Court and 75+ judges [S3][S1]
Self-reports "Exceptionally Well Qualified" ratings from five bar associations (unverified) [S1]

judicial.basis: The only candidate campaigning on programmatic change — therapeutic, community-focused court approaches — rather than solely on readiness and fairness.

safety.basis: 26-year defense/appellate formation combined with an explicit platform of mental-health and substance-use treatment programs to reduce recidivism.

experience.basis: Runs on a proven-record case: full-time judicial work as an ALJ, pro tem service in this court, and the largest claimed slate of judicial endorsers.

sources.ref: PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

candidates.name: Bianca Tse
candidates.summary: Bellevue attorney and King County District Court judge pro tem who spent 16 years as a King County deputy prosecutor trying cases up to homicide, then built a bilingual private practice serving the Asian community. She leads the field in fundraising and her endorsement list leans toward prosecutors, Eastside officials, and sitting judges.
highlights: 16 years as a King County deputy prosecuting attorney, trying homicides, assaults, robberies, and domestic-violence cases [S1][S3]
Judge pro tem since 2021 in King County District Court itself plus the Mercer Island, Kirkland, and Issaquah municipal courts [S1][S2]
Only candidate whose top bar rating has partial third-party corroboration: the Asian Bar Association of Washington's highest designation, per press coverage [S3]
Endorsed per her pamphlet by former Prosecutor Dan Satterberg, Senator Manka Dhingra, and Eastside officials; leads the race in fundraising (~$42,000) [S1][S4]

safety.basis: Professional formation is 16 years of career prosecution including homicide trials, the only prosecution-side formation in the race; no explicit bail or diversion positions documented.

experience.basis: Runs on a readiness-and-record case — long trial career, five years sitting pro tem in this court, sitting-judge encouragement, and the field's largest war chest.

sources.ref: \"Tse eyes run for district court judge\" (April 2026)
PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

contests.office_does: A Seattle City Council district member legislates for the city: passing ordinances, adopting the budget, setting land-use and tax policy, and overseeing departments including police, transportation, and housing.
contests.race_blurb: A special election to fill the District 5 (North Seattle) seat vacated by Cathy Moore, currently held on an interim basis by appointee Debora Juarez. None of the four candidates has held elected office: FairVote organizer Nilu Jenks holds the institutional-progressive endorsement bloc, educator-banker Julie Kang has the Seattle Times and centrist-official backing, democratic socialist Silas James runs the field's left flank, and broker Dimitri Georgakopoulos runs an enforcement-and-business campaign with minimal resources.

candidates.name: Dimitri Georgakopoulos
candidates.summary: Longtime Seattle restaurateur turned Windermere real estate broker running an enforcement-and-business-revitalization campaign as a self-described non-politician. He has minimal fundraising, no endorsements found, and a platform thin on specifics beyond public safety and small-business support.
highlights: Says he will work with the council, mayor, and SPD to keep streets "free of Drug abusers, homeless encampments and violent offenders" [S2]
Focuses on support for District 5 businesses and criticizes bike lanes multiplying "while our commercial corridors go quiet" [S1]
Skeptical of familiar campaign promises including "Tax the rich"; emphasizes accountability from institutions and private-sector partnership [S1]
Nearly three decades as a restaurant owner and 13 years as a real estate broker; describes himself as "not a career politician" [S1]
Minimal campaign infrastructure: $2,050 raised, no endorsements found, not yet qualified for democracy vouchers [S3][S4][S2]

taxes.basis: Explicit skepticism of "Tax the rich" campaign promises, though no detailed tax platform is published.

spending.basis: Emphasizes "accountability from our institutions" and private-sector partnership over program expansion; critiques existing city policies rather than proposing new spending.

safety.basis: The race's most enforcement-oriented platform: keeping streets free of drug users, encampments, and violent offenders, framed around business revitalization rather than services.

experience.basis: Small-business owner who has never held office, runs explicitly as "not a career politician," and operates outside the race's endorsement and fundraising infrastructure.

sources.ref: WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), filer "Theodoros Dimitrios Georgakopoulos (Dimitri Georgakopoulos)", election year 2026
Seattle Ethics and Elections Commission, Democracy Voucher Program participating candidates page

candidates.name: Silas James
candidates.summary: Disabled educator, researcher, and former Seattle Disability Commission chair running as a self-described democratic socialist. His platform — corporate taxes, guaranteed basic income, social housing at 30-40% of stock, and opposition to surveillance contracts — is the furthest left in the race.
highlights: Proposes taxing wealthy corporations, a vacancy tax on residential and commercial property, and a $500/month guaranteed basic income for lower-income households [S1][S2][S4]
Advocates social housing reaching 30-40% of Seattle's housing stock, tenant unions with enforceable penalties, and opposes encampment sweeps [S4][S3]
Frames poverty as the primary public-safety threat; supports restorative justice for nonviolent offenses and CARE-team expansion with independent dispatch authority [S4][S3]
Opposes license-plate readers, CCTV, and the city's Axon contracts, citing cost and ICE connections [S2][S3][S4]
Centers disability access and universal design; raised ~$45,000 with no organizational endorsements found [S1][S3][S5]

taxes.basis: Repeated, platform-central proposals to tax wealthy corporations and vacant properties to fund public services.

spending.basis: Expansion of public programs is his primary lever: guaranteed basic income (submitted to the Council and Mayor), social housing, and corporate-tax-funded community resilience systems.

housing.basis: Social housing at 30-40% of stock, vacancy tax, tenant unions with enforceable penalties, tenants as a protected class, and opposition to sweeps.

safety.basis: Roots public safety in poverty reduction rather than police presence: restorative justice over incarceration, independently dispatched CARE responders, housing-first, and opposition to surveillance tools.

climate.basis: Supports free public transit, dedicated bus and protected bike lanes, and green-jobs development, though climate is not a lead plank.

experience.basis: Self-described democratic socialist outsider with no elected office and no organizational endorsements, running on lived experience navigating benefit systems and advocacy work.

sources.ref: WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), filer "Silas James", election year 2026
Seattle Ethics and Elections Commission, Democracy Voucher Program participating candidates page

candidates.name: Julie Kang
candidates.summary: Seattle University educator, 30-year District 5 resident, and co-founder of a Korean-American community bank, endorsed by the Seattle Times and a coalition spanning centrist councilmembers and Democratic legislators. She pairs rebuilding police staffing with expanded treatment and a permitting-reform housing agenda.
highlights: Top priority pairs rebuilding SPD staffing and a second District 5 precinct with shutting down open-air drug markets and expanding on-demand treatment [S2]
Housing platform centers permitting reform, middle housing, and transit-oriented development, with affordable-housing funding shifted toward payroll-tax revenue [S1][S2]
Suggests combining budget cuts with selective new taxes and describes her decision-making as relying on "data, audits, and lived experience" [S3][S2]
Says she will maintain Seattle's sanctuary-city status and protect immigrant workers and businesses [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times editorial board, the 32nd LD Democrats, and councilmembers from the body's centrist wing; ~$107,000 raised [S5][S1][S2][S6]

taxes.basis: Mixed posture: would combine budget cuts with selective new taxes, and would shift affordable-housing funding onto existing payroll-tax revenue rather than proposing new progressive revenue.

spending.basis: Frames decisions around data and audits, proposes budget cuts alongside selective taxes, and stresses transparent reporting and measurable improvements across city systems.

housing.basis: Leads with market-supply tools — shorter permit review, middle housing, TOD, infrastructure concurrency — while also backing renter displacement protections and public affordable-housing funding.

safety.basis: The field's clearest supporter of rebuilding SPD staffing, a second precinct, and closing open-air drug markets, paired with treatment expansion and behavioral-health investment; sources conflict on SOAP zones.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; runs on a professional educator-and-community-banker background rather than a governmental record.

sources.ref: WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), filer "Mun Julie Kang (Julie Kang)", election year 2026
Seattle Ethics and Elections Commission, Democracy Voucher Program participating candidates page
Seattle City Council blog, "Council names six finalists for District 5 vacancy," 2025-07-17

candidates.name: Nilu Jenks
candidates.summary: FairVote Washington political director, Lake City parent, and first-generation Iranian American making her second run for this seat after finishing third in the 2023 primary. She holds the race's broadest institutional-progressive endorsement base and its largest fundraising total.
highlights: Champions 1,000 new affordable housing units in the district plus just-cause eviction, rent stabilization, and a tenant right to counsel [S4][S1]
Opposes mandatory treatment conditions for shelter; supports housing-first with voluntary treatment and community responder programs, and opposes surveillance expansion [S4][S3]
Professional focus on democracy reform: works at FairVote Washington and prioritizes successful ranked-choice-voting implementation in 2027 [S1][S3]
Climate advocate supporting increased density, protected bike lanes, and transit connections to light rail [S3][S4]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats, three LD Democratic organizations, Working Families Party, multiple unions, and Councilmember Rinck; leads fundraising at ~$109,000 [S1][S2][S5]

spending.basis: Platform is built on expanding public investment — affordable-housing targets, treatment and responder programs, free student transit — without an audit-first framing.

housing.basis: Backs rent stabilization, just-cause eviction, tenant right to counsel, and a 1,000-unit affordable-housing target — the public-investment-and-tenant-protection pole — alongside general density support.

safety.basis: Housing-first with voluntary treatment, community responder programs, opposition to treatment-conditioned shelter, and opposition to surveillance expansion.

climate.basis: Pledges to move the city toward meeting its climate goals and ties her density and transit agenda to them; press describes her as a climate advocate.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office — an organizer/advocate profile — though a repeat candidate and 2025 appointment finalist with sustained district engagement.

reform.basis: Structural political reform is her professional record and a stated priority: FairVote Washington political director committed to implementing ranked choice voting in 2027.

sources.ref: WA PDC contributions data (data.wa.gov dataset kv7h-kjye), filer "Neeloofar Jenks (Nilu Jenks)", election year 2026
Seattle Ethics and Elections Commission, Democracy Voucher Program participating candidates page
Seattle City Council blog, "Council names six finalists for District 5 vacancy," 2025-07-17

contests.office_does: A Seattle Municipal Court judge handles the city's limited-jurisdiction docket — misdemeanors and gross misdemeanors, traffic and parking infractions, and specialty calendars like Veterans Treatment Court — and decides bail, release, and diversion for many residents' only contact with the justice system.
contests.race_blurb: Effectively an open seat with retiring Judge Willie Gregory endorsing Garmon Newsom to succeed him. Lindsay Calkins has withdrawn, leaving a two-way contest with a clean professional split: Newsom's 30-year prosecution and municipal-government career (backed by the Seattle Times and the incumbent) versus Gabe Rothstein's 20-year public-defense career (backed by the King County Democrats and public-defense labor).

candidates.name: Lindsay Calkins
candidates.summary: Trial and appellate attorney with nearly 15 years of litigation experience focused on criminal appeals and resentencing, and the only candidate in the field without pro tem judicial service. She has withdrawn from the race for family reasons, though her statement still appears in the printed pamphlet and her name may remain on the ballot.
highlights: Announced her withdrawal from the race on her campaign website, citing a decision to focus on family [S2]
Nearly 15 years of trial and appellate litigation focused on criminal appeals and resentencing work [S1]
Emphasized access to justice, language access, and courts grounded in the real lives of the people before them [S1]

sources.ref: campaign site now carries a withdrawal announcement
PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

candidates.name: Garmon Newsom
candidates.summary: Seattle Municipal Court pro tem judge and former Burien City Attorney with a 30-plus-year career as a felony and misdemeanor prosecutor, municipal attorney, and civil litigator. He is endorsed by the retiring incumbent and the Seattle Times editorial board and leads the race in fundraising.
highlights: 30+ years in criminal and civil law: Bronx DA and NYC Special Narcotics prosecutor, Renton Chief Prosecutor, Burien City Attorney, and 75+ trials [S1][S2]
Advocates restorative justice and diversion programs that are "evidence-based, effective, and accountable," with courts working with community partners [S1]
Pro tem judicial service at Seattle Municipal Court itself; self-reports "Exceptionally Well-Qualified" ratings from two bar associations (unverified) [S1]
Endorsed by retiring Position 5 incumbent Judge Willie Gregory and the Seattle Times editorial board [S1][S3]
Leads the race in fundraising at roughly $77,000, more than double his opponent [S4]

judicial.basis: Campaigns on restorative justice, community partnership, and access to justice for marginalized communities — a pragmatic, community-responsive framing, qualified by his accountability emphasis.

safety.basis: Genuinely mixed: career professional formation is prosecution and municipal-government law, while his stated platform centers diversion and restorative justice with an evidence-and-accountability caveat; no explicit bail position documented.

experience.basis: The proven-record candidate: three decades of courtroom and public-sector legal leadership, pro tem service in this court, and the retiring incumbent's succession endorsement.

sources.ref: Editorial board recommendation for Seattle Municipal Court Position 5 (June 2026); article paywalled at access time, endorsement confirmed from headline/URL and candidate site
PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

candidates.name: Gabe Rothstein
candidates.summary: Public defender since 2005 with more than 70 trials to verdict, chair of the WSBA Character and Fitness Board, and judge pro tem since 2024 in six regional courts. He is endorsed by the King County Democrats and the SEIU 925 public defense chapter and is the race's most explicit candidate on bail policy.
highlights: 20-year public-defense career including drug-treatment-court attorney, juvenile and civil-commitment unit lead, and 70+ trials to verdict [S1]
States release decisions should be based on flight risk and public safety rather than ability to afford bail [S2]
Prioritizes treatment over punishment in substance-abuse and mental-health cases while balancing victim safety with accountability; serves on the board of an inpatient recovery center [S1][S2]
Judge pro tem since 2024 across six courts, including criminal, jail, and vehicle-impound calendars [S2]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats (independently confirmed), two LD Democratic organizations, and the SEIU 925 Public Defense Chapter [S3][S1]

judicial.basis: Calls Seattle Municipal Court "the people's court" and centers second chances, treatment, and dignity — a community-responsive, reform-open posture on the court's docket.

safety.basis: The race's most explicit prevention-side record: release based on risk rather than bail affordability, treatment over punishment, drug-court and recovery-board service, and a 20-year public-defense formation; medium because he pairs this with victim-safety and accountability language.

experience.basis: Mixed profile: two decades of practice and bar-governance leadership, but judicial service only since 2024 and running against the incumbent-endorsed, editorial-board-backed candidate.

sources.ref: PDC campaign finance summary (data.wa.gov PDC dataset), 2026 election year

measures.title: Annexation of the City of Black Diamond Into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority
measures.what_it_does: Annexes the City of Black Diamond into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority, which would take over fire protection and emergency medical services starting January 1, 2028, replacing the city's expiring contract with Mountain View Fire & Rescue.
measures.cost_line: Adds PSRFA's $1.00/$1,000 property tax levy plus a non-voted fire benefit charge; reported combined example of roughly $1,084/year for a 2,790 sq ft home assessed at $754,000.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Jonathan Englund) says joining PSRFA strengthens fire and EMS service as the community grows, with access to a larger regional system's 24/7 staffing, specialized equipment, and a more stable funding structure than a smaller standalone system.
measures.con_summary: The statement in opposition (Michael Carlin and Brian McGee, a former fire commissioner and career firefighters) says the measure creates a new tax while the city keeps money residents already pay for fire service, estimating about $1,083/year to start, without making response times faster.

taxes.basis: YES adds a permanent PSRFA levy plus fire benefit charge, a significantly higher fire tax than the current sub-$0.50/$1,000 contract cost, to fund regional service.

local-control.basis: YES trades a city-negotiated service contract for membership in a multi-city regional fire authority whose board sets future rates.

measures.title: Formation and Funding of Covington Park and Recreation District
measures.what_it_does: Creates the Covington Park and Recreation District, a new metropolitan park district with the same boundaries as the city, authorized to levy up to $0.75 per $1,000 of assessed value (starting at $0.37) for parks, trails, and recreation facilities, with the city council serving as its board.
measures.cost_line: About $277.50/year (roughly $23/month) on a home assessed at $750,000 at the initial $0.37/$1,000 rate.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Casey Hogan, Kolawole Ogundari, and Laura Morrissey) says parks are falling behind while competing with police and roads for general-fund money, and that 100% of district revenue would be legally dedicated to Covington parks, including replacing the aging aquatic center.
measures.con_summary: The statement in opposition (Gary Cavanaugh) says residents already approved a utility tax tied to parks and are being asked to pay again, that it is not a good time for another new tax, and that the district could charge up to $0.75/$1,000 even though it starts at $0.37.

taxes.basis: YES creates a new junior taxing district with permanent regular property tax authority; the con case is centrally about resisting a new, ratcheting tax.

spending.basis: YES funds expanded park maintenance, improvements, and a planned aquatic/community center — public investment as the lever for amenity outcomes.

measures.title: Property Tax Levy for The Seattle Public Library
measures.what_it_does: Replaces Seattle's expiring 2019 library levy with a seven-year property tax levy collecting $479.8 million from 2027 through 2033 to fund Seattle Public Library operating hours, collections, technology, maintenance, and administration.
measures.cost_line: Adds about $0.23 per $1,000 of assessed value — roughly $193/year for the median homeowner; the pro statement describes it as an additional $9/month for the average homeowner.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Katie B. Wilson, Joy Hollingsworth, and Ken Jennings) says the levy provides more than one third of total library funding, protecting programs, collections, and staffing, and funding seismic, accessibility, and energy upgrades including at the Central Library.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

taxes.basis: YES approves a levy lid lift funding library services — and an enlarged one, expanded about 17% above the mayor's proposal, not a flat renewal.

spending.basis: YES expands programs, seismic retrofits, security, and technology beyond the 2019 levy's baseline — public investment as the lever, with oversight provisions attached.

measures.title: Replacement of Existing Educational Programs and Operations Levy
measures.what_it_does: Authorizes Skykomish School District No. 404 to levy excess property taxes for four years (2027-2030, roughly $165,000 to $192,000 per year), replacing an expiring educational programs and operations levy that funds operations the state does not, such as sports, transportation, technology, and maintenance.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated in the pamphlet; the estimated rate is $0.45 per $1,000 of assessed value, lower than the expiring levy's estimated $0.61.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Sarah Bender, for Skykomish Citizens for Quality Schools) says voting yes keeps classrooms and facilities safe, warm, and dry, funds security components and technology, and describes maintaining the district's historic facilities as fiscally responsible.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

taxes.basis: YES continues taxing to fund school operations, but as a replacement levy at a lower estimated rate than the expiring one it is continuation rather than a new or larger tax.

measures.jurisdiction: King County Fire Protection District No. 43
measures.title: Annexation of the District into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority
measures.what_it_does: Annexes King County Fire Protection District No. 43 (Maple Valley/Hobart area) into the Puget Sound Regional Fire Authority, which already serves the district by contract; the district would stop levying its own taxes and residents would instead pay the RFA's taxes and benefit charges, gaining board representation and a vote on future RFA measures.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated; the pamphlet does not itemize post-annexation rates for the district.
measures.pro_summary: The statement in favor (Andrew Montero, Warren Iverson) says annexation changes nothing about who responds to 911 calls but guarantees two seats on the RFA board and the right to vote on future RFA tax and benefit-charge measures the community currently has no say in.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only one side.

local-control.basis: YES folds an independent local district into a large regional authority for long-term stability, though it formalizes an existing service relationship and adds voting representation rather than changing who delivers service.

measures.jurisdiction: King County Fire Protection District No. 47
measures.title: Property Tax Levy for Fire Protection and Emergency Medical Services
measures.what_it_does: Restores King County Fire Protection District No. 47's (Ravensdale area) regular property tax levy from about $0.54 to $1.00 per $1,000 of assessed value, with CPI-indexed growth allowed through 2031 and the lifted level made permanent in the levy base, to maintain current fire and EMS service levels.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated in the pamphlet; the increase is $0.46 per $1,000 of assessed value (for example, $46/year per $100,000 of assessed value).
measures.pro_summary: No statement in favor was filed; the district's official explanatory statement says the board determined the restoration is necessary to maintain current fire protection and EMS service levels and support firefighter safety.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only the district's own explanation.

taxes.basis: YES is a multi-year levy lid lift raising the property tax rate by $0.46/$1,000 with a permanently reset levy base to fund fire and EMS services.

measures.title: Intent to Continue Voter Authorized Benefit Charge
measures.what_it_does: Continues Snoqualmie Pass Fire and Rescue's existing benefit charge — a fee scaled to structure size and risk rather than assessed value — for six more years, replacing the expiring charge voters approved in 2020; while in effect, the district gives up $0.50/$1,000 of property tax authority.
measures.cost_line: Cost to a typical household not stated; the charge is set annually per property based on building size and risk, capped at 60% of the district's operating budget.
measures.pro_summary: No statement in favor was filed; the district's official explanatory statement presents the board's view that a benefit charge paired with lower property taxes is the fairest, most stable, and most cost-effective way to finance district operations.
measures.con_summary: No statement against this measure was filed — no one in the jurisdiction came forward to serve on the con committee, so the pamphlet presents only the district's own explanation.

measures.title: Emergency Medical Services Property Tax Levy
measures.what_it_does: Re-authorizes regular property tax levies of $0.50 or less per $1,000 of assessed valuation for six consecutive years to continue emergency medical services.
measures.cost_line: Renews an EMS levy up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.

taxes.basis: YES renews a local property tax levy for emergency medical services.

measures.title: Emergency Medical Care and Services
measures.what_it_does: Renews Fircrest's authority to impose regular property tax levies of up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed valuation for emergency medical care and services for six consecutive years.
measures.cost_line: Renews an EMS levy up to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.

taxes.basis: YES renews a local property tax levy for emergency medical care and services.

measures.title: Funding Transportation Safety Improvements
measures.what_it_does: Funds street, sidewalk, route, pothole, paving, maintenance, traffic safety, and neighborhood connection improvements through utility and property tax increases for 10 years.
measures.cost_line: Adds a 1.5% utility tax and raises the regular property tax levy by $0.20 per $1,000 for 2027 collections, with subsequent levies through 2036 based on the 2027 amount.

taxes.basis: YES raises utility and property taxes for transportation safety improvements.

measures.title: Emergency Medical Services Levy Lid Lift
measures.what_it_does: Restores Everett's emergency medical services levy to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value in 2027 and 2028 for emergency medical care, paramedic services, and related expenses.
measures.cost_line: Restores the EMS levy rate to $0.50 per $1,000 of assessed value.

taxes.basis: YES raises/restores a property tax levy for emergency medical services.

measures.title: Sales and Use Tax for Enhanced Public Safety Services
measures.what_it_does: Imposes a 0.1% sales and use tax for public safety and criminal justice purposes, including police staffing, dispatch, courts, prosecution, public defense, jail services, and related support.
measures.cost_line: Adds a one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) sales and use tax.

taxes.basis: YES adds a local sales and use tax for public safety services.

measures.title: Renewal of Residential Street Utility Tax
measures.what_it_does: Renews Cheney's tax on electrical energy and natural gas businesses to 16.75% of gross revenue for 14 years, with proceeds used to repair streets and sidewalks.
measures.cost_line: Renews a utility tax rate six percentage points above the otherwise authorized 10.75% rate for 14 years.

taxes.basis: YES renews a utility tax for street and sidewalk repair.
